title: Pricing Implications of Noise
abstract: We study the interaction between noisy demand and skewed asset payoffs. In our model, price as a function of quantities is convex in a neighborhood around zero if and only if skewness is positive. The combination of convexity and noise produces the idiosyncratic skewness effect, a documented negative relationship between an asset’s idiosyncratic skewness and its expected return. We further offer an explanation for the idiosyncratic volatility puzzle. Finally, our theory predicts that higher idiosyncratic skewness strengthens the idiosyncratic volatility effect (and vice versa). We find support for this prediction in the cross-section of stock returns.

author.Xingtan Zhang: Cheung Kong Graduate School of Business (CKGSB) and University of Colorado Boulder, USA

Noise is important in financial markets. As noted byGrossman and Stiglitz (1980),Black (1986), and others, noise is essential to the existence of liquid markets, since it breaks the no-trade theorem ofMilgrom and Stokey (1982). Hence, noise trading (because of exogenous liquidity demand or belief errors) is a standard assumption when studying informed trading.1

In this paper, we study a different implication of noise. Specifically, we explore the interaction of noise trading with asymmetrically distributed payoffs. We find that with the presence of noise, the expected price (expected excess return) can deviate away from fundamental value (zero), with the direction of the deviation controlled by the direction of payoff asymmetry, that is, skewness. Moreover, our model can produce the idiosyncratic volatility puzzle. Finally, our theory generates the novel prediction that a higher value of idiosyncratic skewness strengthens the idiosyncratic volatility effect (and vice versa).

In our model, there is a representative rational investor and a single risky asset in zero net supply.2There are also noise traders with perfectly inelastic demand drawn from an arbitrary mean-zero distribution.3Trading occurs at time 0, and the asset pays a liquidating dividend at time 1 with meanμ⁠. We define the fundamental value of the asset as the price that obtains when noise demand is identically zero. This will be the expected payoff,μ⁠, since the asset’s net supply is zero.

The representative investor is a price taker with a flexible utility function. We only assume that it is strictly increasing, strictly concave, thrice continuously differentiable, and exhibits nonincreasing absolute risk aversion (NARA). The assumption of NARA ensures that a risky asset with a positive expected return is not an inferior good, that is, wealthier investors allocate weakly more money to the risky asset.4Note that the NARA class nests both constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) and constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) preferences.Arditti (1967)shows that NARA is sufficient to imply a preference for positive skewness. One of our contributions is showing that NARA also implies that the investor’s demand is convex in a neighborhood around the fundamental value,μ⁠, if and only if the payoff skewness is positive.

Figure 1provides some graphical intuition for the relationship between skewness and convexity. Start with the demand function for an asset with some distribution, given by the thinner curve in the figure.5Now consider an otherwise identical asset with higher skewness, with demand given by the thicker curve.6Since a NARA investor has a preference for skewness, demand for this new asset must be weakly higher at any price. However, when price equals fundamental value,μ⁠, demand for any asset is zero. As a result, the thicker curve is more “curved” than the thinner curve in the neighborhood aroundp=μ⁠; increasing skewness increases the demand function’s convexity.

Figure 1 Higher skewness leads to a more convex demand curve This figure illustrates that ceteris paribus, higher skewness leads to a more convex demand curve. The thicker curve represents the demand associated with a higher skewness, while the thinner curve represents the demand associated with a lower skewness. Since a NARA investor has a preference for skewness, the thicker curve is weakly above the thinner curve for any price. In the meantime, when the price is equal to fundamental value, demand is precisely zero, regardless of skewness. Put differently, both curves must cross the price axis at the fundamental value. As a result, the thicker curve is more “curved” than the thinner curve in the neighborhood around μ ⁠ . Open in new tab Download slide

By market clearing, the representative investor takes the exact negative position of the noise traders. Solving for price as a function of noise, it is simply the investor’s inverse demand function. Given downward sloping demand, the price function exactly inherits the convexity of the demand function. To understand the interaction between convexity and noise, suppose the asset payoff is positively skewed, so NARA investors’ demand function is convex around the fundamental value. Consider a simple noise distribution wherenoisy demandis±ηwith equal probability, as shown inFigure 2. Because of convexity, the upward price deviation caused whennoiseis+ηis greater than the downward deviation caused whennoiseis−η⁠. Therefore, the average price (integrated over the noise distribution) is higher than the fundamental value. Similarly, concavity leads to downward deviations in the average price. Since returns are inversely related to prices, we obtain similar deviations in their expectation, except with the opposite sign. As a summary, an econometrician who observes realized returns over time in repeated instances of this simple setting (even if the details of the market participants are not observable) would document a negative expected return for a positively skewed asset, namely, the skewness effect.

Figure 2 Demand for a positively skewed asset Since buying a positively skewed asset entails more desirable upside risk, whereas shorting the asset involves more downside risk, the risk premium investors demand for accommodating negative noisy demand is lower in magnitude than that required for positive. This manifests in an average price that is higher than fundamental value. Open in new tab Download slide

Having qualitatively established the skewness effect, we next explore what factors can strengthen or weaken the phenomenon. To do that, we employ a Taylor series expansion to obtain the dominating terms in the expected price and expected excess return. We show that the deviation of expected price from the fundamental value and expected excess return from zero are approximately proportional to the cube of the volatility of the bet, the skewness of the bet, and the variance of the noise, as given by the following equations: 7 E [ Price ] − Fundamental Value ∝ + Volatility 3 × Skew × var(Noise) , (1) E [ Excess Return ] ∝ − Volatility 3 × Skew × var(Noise) . (2)

First, note the opposite signs, which are intuitive since prices and returns are inversely related. Second, noise strengthens the skewness effect–the higher the variance of noise, the larger the deviation. Furthermore, Equations (1) and (2) remind us that the presence of noise is crucial to drive our results. If there were no noise, the right-hand side of Equations (1) and (2) would be zero, implying no deviation regardless of the level of skewness.

Given that most stocks have positive expected idiosyncratic skewness (Boyer, Mitton, and Vorkink 2010), our theory offers an explanation for the idiosyncratic volatility puzzle.8To see this, differentiate Equation (2) with respect to volatility and observe the sign is negative if and only if skewness is positive. Since idiosyncratic skewness is typically positive, higher idiosyncratic volatility leads to lower expected returns.

Equations (1) and (2) also suggest that the idiosyncratic volatility and idiosyncratic skewness phenomena interact. A higher value of idiosyncratic volatility strengthens the idiosyncratic skewness effect (and vice versa). To our knowledge, this prediction is novel to the theoretical literature on idiosyncratic moments and expected returns. We provide supporting evidence for this prediction in Section3using an independent double sort of stocks on expected idiosyncratic volatility and skewness. Using a conditional sort as inAng et al. (2006), we show that after controlling for the third moment,τ=Volatility3×Skewness⁠, there is no longer a relationship between returns and either skewness or volatility, consistent with Equation (2). Another novel prediction coming from Equation (2) is an interaction betweenτandvariance(Noise)⁠; a higher value of one increases the return affect associated with the other. Again using an independent sort, we find strong empirical support for this prediction.

Finally, our result may be surprising in light of the findings inKraus and Litzenberger (1976). That paper also assumes NARA preferences and finds that covariance and coskewness with the market portfolio are relevant for valuation. In a multiasset extension, we show that our main results hold for an asset whose payoff is independent of the market portfolio. Therefore we find an important role foridiosyncraticvolatility and skewness. The key reason for this stark difference is that noise creates a time-varying wedge between the market portfolio and that of the marginal investor. Conditional on the realization of noise, there is a Kraus-Litzenberger-type pricing model that holds. Specifically, the conditional expected return on any asset is pinned down by its covariance and coskewness with theinvestor’s portfolio. However, the presence of noise means the investor does not hold the market.

Our paper is related to the literature on how skewness affects asset prices. Early research on the impact of skewness on investors’ decisions recognized that since investors prefer upside risk to downside risk, they will choose portfolios that are more positively skewed, all else being equal (Arditti 1967;Scott and Horvath 1980). In related studies,Rubenstein (1973),Kraus and Litzenberger (1976,1983), andHarvey and Siddique (2000)incorporated these observations into coskewness models that focused on higher moment contributions to stochastic discount factors.9These models imply no role for idiosyncratic skewness in pricing.

However, a recent and growing literature has documented a negative relationship between ex ante skewness of individual stocks and their subsequent expected returns, which is unexplained by standard risk-based asset pricing models, such as the capital asset pricing model (CAPM), theFama and French (1993)three-factor model, or coskewness factors.

Ex ante skewness has been difficult to measure (Harvey and Siddique 2000), but the skewness effect has been demonstrated recently using several different and effective measures:Boyer, Mitton, and Vorkink (2010)use firm characteristics and cross-sectional regressions to construct a measure of predicted skewness for individual stocks;Amaya et al. (2015)construct a measure of realized skewness (as an analog to realized volatility) using high-frequency returns data;Conrad et al. (2013)construct an options-implied ex ante skewness measure; andBoyer and Vorkink (2014)demonstrate the skewness effect for returns on equity options using a measure based on moneyness and return volatility.

Theoretical explanations consistent with this recent evidence have invoked nonstandard preferences, such as cumulative prospect theory (Barberis and Huang 2008), non-Bayesian beliefs (Brunnermeier and Parker 2005;Brunnermeier et al. 2007), or heterogeneous preferences (Mitton and Vorkink 2007). These alternative explanations may indeed apply to price formation in real markets; however, we show how the skewness effect can arise without such ingredients. Our prediction of an interaction between the idiosyncratic volatility and idiosyncratic skewness phenomena is unique to this literature.

Our paper is related to a large literature on the idiosyncratic volatility puzzle. In their influential paper,Ang et al. (2006)show a negative relationship between an asset’s idiosyncratic volatility and its expected return. This finding is puzzling because traditional asset pricing theories either predict no relation or predict a positive relationship.10Many papers have subsequently tried to explain the puzzle. SeeHou and Loh (2016)for a list of candidate explanations. Most papers in this literature empirically show that the puzzle can be explained by lottery preferences of investors and/or various forms of market frictions.11We contribute to this literature by offering a theoretical explanation based on two well-founded mild assumptions (i.e., NARA utility and the presence of noise).
In this section, we present a simple model to understand our main results. While this section studies a single risky asset, in Section2we show that our main predictions generalize to a setting with multiple risky assets. The key result is that the convexity/concavity of the equilibrium price function is closely linked to the skewness of the underlying risky asset’s payoff.

1.1 Model setup

Consider a two-period financial market,t∈{0,1}⁠. There is a risk-free asset, with both its price and payoff normalized to one, and a single risky asset, with time-0 pricep~and time-1 payoffθ~⁠. We assume thatVar(θ~)is strictly positive to avoid a trivial solution. Note that the payoffθ~is exogenously given, while the pricep~will be endogenously determined by market clearing.

There is a single representative agent (investor) in the financial market. Att=0⁠, the investor submits a demand schedulex(p)⁠, which specifies how many shares of the risky asset she would buy or sell at pricep⁠. We assume that the agent has a von Neumann-Morgenstern utility functionu(w)and initial wealthw0⁠. The utility functionu(⋅)is strictly increasing, strictly concave, and thrice continuously differentiable. We further assume the utility function exhibits nonincreasing absolute risk aversion (NARA), ensuring that risky assets with positive returns are not inferior goods. As shown in the literature (e.g.,Arditti 1967, among many others), NARA implies a strictly positive third derivative:u‴(⋅)>0⁠.12

Lastly, and central to our analysis, we assume noisy demand (or unmodeled liquidity demand) for the risky asset. Unlike in the noisy rational expectations equilibrium (REE) literature, noise in our model serves as a wedge between the investor’s and econometrician’s information sets, not between the information sets of various investors (seeGrossman and Stiglitz 1980;Admati 1985, among others). Noise is denoted by−η~⁠, which is a zero-mean random variable with strictly positive variance. We further assume that the supply of the risky asset is zero, so the market clearing condition isx(p)−η~=0⁠. The price of the risky asset is set to clear the market for each realization ofη~⁠. We show in Section2.2that our results are robust to nonzero supply.

Equilibrium.An equilibrium consists of a pair(x(p),p(η))such that the demand schedulex(p)solves the representative agent’s expected utility maximization problem and the price functionp(η)clears the market when the realization ofη~isη⁠.

1.2 Model analysis

We obtain Lemma 1 from a Taylor expansion ofx(p)aroundp=μ⁠. We relegate the proof of the lemma to the appendix. We make a few remarks. First, as we can see from the lemma, the slope of the demand function atp=μis given byx′(μ)=u′(w0)u″(w0)1σ2⁠, which is negative becauseu″<0⁠. Consider a special case when the utility function is given by a constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) utility function and the payoff of the underlying risky assetθ~is normally distributed. In this case, skewness is zero, and the quadratic term in Equation (5) is zero, consistent with the well-known result that the demand function is linear under CARA and normality.

Second, the quadratic term in Equation (5) captures the curvature of the demand function atp=μ⁠. The sign ofx″(μ)clearly depends solely ons⁠, since the utility function has a negative second derivative and a positive third derivative. Specifically, ifs>0⁠, thenx″(μ)>0⁠; ifs<0⁠, thenx″(μ)<0⁠. Lemma 1 states that a NARA investor’s demand of a positively (negatively) skewed risky asset is strictly convex (concave) atp=μ⁠. While the formal proof is in the appendix,Figure 3offers a graphical intuition for the result. Recall that a NARA investor has a preference for positive skewness. This fact implies that when we increase the skewness of the underlying asset, holding everything else equal, the investor would demand weakly more shares of the risky asset. That is, the demand function is weakly higher, consistent with the observation that the solid curve is above the dashed line inFigure 3.13Furthermore, as we discussed above, the demand has to be zero whenp=μbecause of the FOC (4). As a result, the “curvature” of the demand function of a positively skewed asset atp=μhas to be greater than the “curvature” of a straight line. Since the curvature of a straight line is zero, the curvature of the solid curve atp=μhas to be positive.

Third, Lemma 1 concerns the behavior of the demand function in a neighborhood aroundp=μ⁠. A natural question is what is the shape of the demand function at other prices? In Section2.2.1, we show that it is generally impossible to obtain global convexity/concavity, and a simple setting with binary payoffs is provided as a counterexample. In Section2.2.2, we discuss the behavior of the demand function when there is nonzero supply, which implies that the price may no longer be “close” toμ⁠. We show that a general result on convexity/concavity can be obtained for a sufficiently skewed asset.

Finally, we comment on our approach of taking a Taylor series expansion of the demand function instead of expanding expected utility.14Of course, the demand function is derived from the expected utility maximization problem according to the FOC (4). The drawback of the standard approach is that taking the Taylor series of expected utility using the first three moments implies that optimal demand solves a quadratic equation. However, the quadratic equation may not have a real solution, even though we can prove that the FOC always does. Hence, taking a Taylor series on the demand function directly ensures the approximation yields an equilibrium. In Section2.3, we follow the standard approach when analyzing the case of multiple risky assets and CRRA (power) utility.

To complete the equilibrium analysis, we need to find the price functionp(η)⁠. From the market clearing condition when the realization ofη~isη⁠, we setx(p)−η=0⁠. It follows thatx(p(η))=η⁠. Put differently, the price function is the inverse of the demand function. Given that Lemma 1 characterizes the behavior of the demand function whenpis close toμ⁠, we obtain a similar result for the price function, as summarized in the following proposition.

Proposition 1 If the underlying risky asset is positively (negatively) skewed, then the price function p ( η ) is convex (concave) around η = 0 ⁠ . Furthermore, there exists an η ― > 0 such that if the noisy demand is bounded by η ― > 0 ⁠ , then

The first part of Proposition 1 states that in a neighborhood of zero noise demand, the price function for a positively (negatively) skewed asset is convex (concave). The second part follows from applying Jensen’s inequality to the price function. Importantly, it offers an empirically testable prediction because it relates two potentially observable quantities: skewness and expected price. More specifically, an econometrician who observes the market clearing price over time in repeated instances of this simple setting (even if the details of the market participants are not observable) will document an average market price ofE[p(η~)]⁠. Proposition 1 states that higher skewness would push up the expected price, and a positively skewed asset has an expected price higher than the expected payoffμ⁠.

The presence of noise is key. If there were no noisy demand, given the zero supply assumption, the price of the asset is necessarily equal to the expected payoff, regardless of skewness. This observation differentiates our work from the literature that studies the relationship between skewness and prices. In a multiasset framework,Kraus and Litzenberger (1976)show that a security’s price is determined by its covariance and coskewness with market, under the assumption of no noise. The expected price in Proposition 1 can be thought of as the risk-adjusted expected price after controlling for systematic risk, that is, covariance and coskewness. So the message of our model is that even if we control for “systematic risk,” the higher the asset’s skewness, the higher the expected price. We discuss this point in further detail in Section2.3.

Most of the noisy rational expectations literature uses CARA preferences and assets with normally distributed payoffs and finds price to be linear in noise. Linearity implies that noise does not affect expected prices.15However, we show that the underlying risky asset’s skewness determines the shape of a NARA investor’s demand function. In particular, price as a function of noise is nonlinear for an asymmetrically-distributed asset. Thus, noise matters in our model despite symmetric information.

Since empirical research largely studies returns instead of expected prices, we next make predictions regarding the risky asset’s return. Throughout the main text, we studydollar excess returns,R~=θ~−p(η~)⁠, as is common in the Noisy Rational Expectations Equilibrium (NREE) literature.16In AppendixB, we analyze rates of returnr~=θ~/p(η~)−1⁠, and we show that our results are robust to this change.

Corollary 1 delivers an empirically testable prediction. That is, a stock with sufficiently positive skewness can earn a negative expected return. This prediction stands in sharp contrast to the existing literature (e.g.,Kraus and Litzenberger 1976), which argues that coskewness is the relevant pricing factor. Note that skewness in our model isidiosyncraticskewness, because the risky asset has zero net supply. Our single-risky-asset model reveals that idiosyncratic skewness matters for pricing. We show in Section2that a similar result obtains with multiple risky assets.

1.3 Comparative statics

In the previous section, we showed that depending on the risky asset’s skewness, its expected price can deviate from its expected payoff, and its expected return can deviate from zero. In this section, we further analyze how various asset parameters affect these deviations.

First, following the proof of Proposition 1, we obtain closed-form expressions of the dominant term for expected returns, as summarized by the following corollary.

Corollary 2 The expected dollar excess return is given by the following expression. E ( R ~ ) = − 1 2 u ‴ ( w 0 ) u ′ ( w 0 ) σ 3 s V a r ( η ~ ) + o ( 1 ) ∗ V a r ( η ~ ) , where o ( 1 ) is an unknown function that converges to 0 as max { | η ~ | } goes to zero.

We write the dominant term in the expected dollar excess return as E R ― := − 1 2 u ‴ ( w 0 ) u ′ ( w 0 ) σ 3 s V a r ( η ~ ) . (7)

Corollary 2 further gives a functional form for the deviation involving the product of idiosyncratic volatility, skewness, and noise variance. Using the dominant-term expression, (7), we can analyze comparative statics with respect to various parameters. The following proposition summarizes the results, all of which immediately follow via differentiation of Equation (7).

Proposition 2 There exists an η ― such that if noisy demand is bounded by η ― > 0 ⁠ , then the expected dollar excess return satisfies the following properties. (The skewness effect): The expected return is decreasing in skewness. (The volatility effect): If skewness is positive, the expected return is decreasing in volatility. Higher volatility strengthens the skewness effect, and vice versa. (The τ effect): Let τ be the expected third central moment, τ := E [ ( θ ~ − μ ) 3 ] = σ 3 s . The expected return is decreasing in τ ⁠ . (The noise effect): If τ is positive, the expected return is decreasing in the variance of noise, V a r ( η ~ ) ⁠ . Higher variance of noise strengthens the τ effect, and vice versa.

We make a few remarks. First,Ang et al. (2006)and others study idiosyncratic volatility, that is, the volatility of residuals after controlling for systematic risk factors. Since the risky asset in our model is assumed to be in zero net supply, well-established risks like covariance and coskewness with the market are identically zero. Hence, volatility and skewness in the above formulas are “idiosyncratic.” In Section2.3we allow for an arbitrary number of positive net supply assets and assume that the payoff of the skewed asset being analyzed is independent of the market return. In that setting, all of the results in Proposition 2 go through.

Second, almost all empirical papers look at the volatility of percent returns instead of dollar returns. In our model, the volatility of percent returns isVar(θ~p(η~))⁠, whose Taylor expansion (see AppendixBfor the proof) isσ2μ2+O(1)Var(η~),where the big-O notation represents an unknown term that is a constant as the magnitude of noise goes to zero. Since we consider relatively small (bounded) noisy demand, there is a one-to-one mapping between the payoff’s volatility and the return volatility. Hence, all comparative statics results apply to the volatility of percent returns as well.

Finally, noise volatility strengthens all effects, including the skewness effect, the volality effect, and theτeffect. We make two remarks. First, the assumption of noise/liquidity demand is crucial for the skewness effect. As we can see fromFigure 6, if the volatility of noise shrinks to zero, expected price converges to the expected payoffμ⁠, in which case there is no skewness or volatility effects. Second, the comparative static with respect to noise provides an empirically testable prediction. As we increase the size of noise as inFigure 6, expected price is higher and expected return is lower. In Section 4.4, we find support for this prediction in the cross-section of stock returns.

article.2. Discussion:
In this section, we explore three model extensions: (1) heterogeneous investors, (2) nonzero asset supply, and (3) multiple risky assets.

2.1 Heterogeneous investors

Our baseline model features a representative investor, but this is not a crucial assumption. In particular, we show that the convexity or concavity property of the aggregate demand function does not rely on the existence of a representative agent. The simple intuition is that the second derivative operator is linear.

To fix ideas, suppose there are two NARA investors, and the underlying risky asset is positively skewed so that each NARA investor’s demand is convex atp=μaccording to Lemma 1. The second derivative of the aggregate demand atp=μis equal to the sum of the second derivative of each investor’s demand function atp=μ⁠. Given that each investor’s demand is convex atp=μ⁠, the aggregate demand function is also convex atp=μ⁠.

The above arguments work as long as each investor in the economy has a NARA utility function, regardless of the number of investors in the economy. Once we establish the convexity/concavity of the aggregate demand function, if we introduce noisy demand, the expected price can be higher/lower than the expected payoff as determined by Jensen’s inequality. So, our results are robust with heterogeneous investors, even if commonly used aggregation assumptions, such as those inCass and Stiglitz (1970), fail.

2.2 Global convexity/concavity?

The convexity/concavity property of the demand function in Lemma 1 is the key to all our predictions regarding expected price and expected return. A natural question is whether the demand function can begloballyconvex or concave under certain conditions.

In this section, we present two sets of results under the assumption that the risky asset’s payoff is bounded. First, we show a negative result that it is generally impossible to obtain a globally convex/concave demand function. So, in this sense, the “local” convexity/concavity in Lemma 1 is tight.

Second, “local” convexity/concavity may impose limitations to our results if the relevant price is not close to the expected payoff, for instance, when there is a positive aggregate supply. To deal with this concern, we show a positive result that given any aggregate supplyχ⁠, the demand function is convex at the price such thatx(p)=χ⁠, provided skewness is high enough. Consequently, our predictions on expected price and expected return are qualitatively robust.

2.2.1 An impossibility result

In this subsection, we show an impossibility result that the demand function can not be globally convex/concave under the assumption that the risky asset’s payoff is bounded. We then explicitly solve a simple example to illustrate the result. Furthermore, the example can be helpful to understand the positive result in the next subsection.

We first show that the demand function can not be globally convex/concave. We provide some intuition. Suppose the risky asset’s payoff is bounded above byθ―and below byθ―⁠. By a no-arbitrage argument, the equilibrium price must be bounded byθ―andθ―⁠. Moreover, the representative investor’s demand approaches to negative infinity as the price approaches the upper boundθ―⁠:limp→θ―x(p)=−∞⁠. Similarly,limp→θ―x(p)=+∞⁠. Graphically, it is impossible to draw a globally convex or concave demand function connecting negative infinity to positive infinity in a bounded interval. AppendixBpresents the formal proof. We use a specific model to illustrate the impossibility result.

In this subsection, we prove a general result showing that despite the nonglobal convexity/concavity, our results are robust to nonzero supply.

2.3 Multiple risky assets

So far, we have presented a model with a single risky asset, and our main finding is that a positively skewed asset can earn a negative expected return. However, it is not obvious how our results relate toKraus and Litzenberger (1976)(hereafter KL), who show that coskewness with the market, rather than idiosyncratic skewness, is relevant for valuation. To address this natural concern, we extend the baseline model to multiple risky assets. Our approach parallels that ofBarberis and Huang (2008)in the addition of zero net supply, independent, skewed assets to a standard multiple security economy. We show that our results can not be fully explained by KL, and idiosyncratic skewness matters for valuation in our model.

In the following, we assume that the representative investor has a constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) utility function. We show that the multiple risky assets problem is separable in the sense that the equilibrium price of the skewed asset coincides with the equilibrium price in the baseline model with a single risky asset. In AppendixB, we show that our results hold with a CRRA utility function using the same Taylor expansion as in KL.

To fix ideas, suppose the representative investor has CARA utility over final wealth w (i.e., − e − α w ⁠ ) with risk aversion parameter α > 0 ⁠ . We write θ ~ M as the payoff to the market portfolio, and θ ~ is the payoff to the skewed asset of interest. For simplicity, we assume noisy demand − η ~ in the skewed asset and no noisy demand in all other assets. Both noisy demand and the risky asset payoff are assumed to be independent of all other random variables. The investor’s problem is max x − E [ exp ⁡ ( − α ( w 0 + x ( p ) ( θ ~ − p ) + x M ( p M ) ( θ ~ M − p M ) ) ] , where x ( p ) is the investor’s demand for the skewed asset and x ( p M ) is the demand for the market portfolio. Since θ ~ M is independent of θ ~ and η ~ ⁠ , we can express the problem as max x − E [ exp ⁡ ( − α ( w 0 + x ( p ) ( θ ~ − p ) ) ] × E [ exp ⁡ ( − α x M ( p M ) ( θ ~ M − p M ) ) ] . Since we can integrate out the second term, the demand of the skewed asset is pinned down by the first term, max x − E [ exp ⁡ ( − α ( w 0 + x ( p ) ( θ ~ − p ) ) ] , which is precisely the problem we analyzed in Section 2. So all our results in Section 2 carry over. In particular, a positively skewed asset can earn a negative expected return, even if its payoff is independent from the market portfolio.

One may wonder why we obtain this result, since CARA and CRRA are special cases of NARA utility (the assumption in KL). The key is noise. Consider some realization of noisy demand,−η⁠. The representative investor’s portfolio consists of the market plusη⁠. Using the return on this portfolio, conditional expected returns on all assets would be given by KL’s pricing model. That is, the expected return on any asset (including the skewed asset being studied) is determined by its covariance and coskewness with the investor’s portfolio. Note however, that the investor does not hold the market! Hence, even a conditional coskewness model using the market portfolio would fail. In conclusion, our results do not contradict the main findings of KL. The difference comes because noise creates a time-varying wedge between the market portfolio and that of the marginal investor. Convexity of the demand function implies that when we average over the noise distribution, the unconditional expected return of the positively skewed asset is negative though its payoff is independent of the market.
article.3. Empirical Test:
In this section, we test the key empirical predictions of the model. First, the model generates the previously documented idiosyncratic skewness effect—high skewness is correlated with lowerα⁠. Next the model generates the known idiosyncratic variance effect—high variance is correlated with lowerα⁠. Finally, the model predicts an interaction between the two—a higher value idiosyncratic skewness strengthens the idiosyncratic variance effect (and vice versa). Our key empirical strategy is to construct portfolios double-sorted on idiosyncratic variance and skewness. We then examine whetherαs follow the above predicted patterns. Using a similar double sort, we also test the predicted interaction betweenτand variance(noise).

3.1 Data

We obtain predicted idiosyncratic skewness from Brian Boyer’s website, constructed as inBoyer, Mitton, and Vorkink (2010).17The basic method involves a Fama-Macbeth-style panel regression to predict realized idiosyncratic skewness. The sample runs from January 1987 to December 2016.

We construct predicted idiosyncratic variance in a similar way. First, each calendar quarter we calculate the variance ofFama and French(1993, henceforth FF3F) residuals for each stock. Next, each day using the implied volatility surface files from Options Metrics, we calculate the price of a 91 day straddle with strike equal to the forward price (also from Options Metrics). Recall that the price of this portfolio is exactly the risk-neutral mean absolute deviation of the 91-day return. Under normality, it is closely linked to volatility by the standard formula,E(|x|)=σ2π⁠. To remove scale effects, we divide this price by the current stock price, giving the risk-neutral expected absolute deviation of returns. FollowingBoyer, Mitton, and Vorkink (2010), we average this measure over the calendar quarter. One advantage of using mean absolute deviation instead of variance is that for many stocks, liquid options exist at only a few strikes. Computing variance theoretically requires strikes ranging from zero to infinity. Our simpler method only requires traded options with strikes near the current stock price. Finally, we predict the logarithm of realized variance in quarterq+1with the logarithm of realized variance and the logarithm of the option-implied measure from quarterq⁠. FollowingBoyer, Mitton, and Vorkink (2010), we run this cross-sectional regression separately each quarter and then average the estimated coefficients over time. Finally, we use these average coefficients to construct forecasts that we then use to conditionally sort stocks.Figure C1in the appendix gives the cross-sectional predictiveR2⁠. They average above 70%and are consistently high, even during the dot-com era.

Since the Options Metrics data begins in 1996, our final sample runs from 1996Q1 to 2016Q4. Based on predicted idiosyncratic volatility and skewness, we perform an independent double sort into terciles each quarter. We use these portfolio assignments to construct value-weighted monthly returns on nine portfolios.18The realized portfolio returns run from April 1996 to March 2017.

3.2 Skewness and volatility interaction

In testing the interaction between volatility and skewness via a double sort, it is vital to first obtain independent variation in both moments. That is, the ideal test is to obtain cross-sectional variation in each moment that is uncorrelated with the other.Tables 1and2show that the double sort into terciles achieves this desideratum. For each portfolio, each month, we compute the average predicted idiosyncratic volatility and skewness. We then average these measures over time. First, looking across the rows ofTable 1, one sees that as skewness increases, volatility stays constant. Next, looking down columns ofTable 2, as volatility increases skewness stays constant. Hence, it is a “fair” comparison to look at the high-low volatility return spread for low and high skew portfolios. Similarly, we can reasonably compare the high-low skewness return spread for low and high volatility portfolios.

As usual, this brings back the joint hypothesis problem: which factors to include? Assuming no arbitrage, there exists some factor model that “explains” all patterns in expected returns. Besides the market, the other factors are only loosely (if at all) microfounded. As shown in the introduction ofFama and French (2015), variation in expected returns (for any reason) must manifest in size, book-to-market, profitability, and investment. It is almost tautological given the present value relation and clean surplus accounting. Hence, we consider the CAPM as the most appropriate microfounded benchmark. 3.4 Noise

Finally, Proposition 2 also predicts an interaction effect between the third moment,τand the variance of noise demand. One difficulty with testing this hypothesis is that noise trading is inherently difficult to observe. As noted inFang et al. (2020), “[t]ruly random buys and sells by noise traders are uninformed but difficult to capture. Other potential proxies—retail and small trades—are shown to be sometimes informed, making them less-than-ideal measures of noise trading." They propose an advertising based instrument for noise aimed at alleviating this concern. Unfortunately, their measure relies on proprietary data that only begin in 2009. Measures that use social media or Google trends likewise have short histories.21The news-based measure of media sentiment ofTetlock (2007)only applies to the aggregate market. Finally, mutual fund flows or measuring changes in retail ownership from 13F filings only yield quarterly observations of flows, too low frequency to measure the volatility of noise trading.

In the absence of direct measurement, one must indirectly measure noise through the lens of a model. In AppendixB, we present a simple dynamic model of noise trading that implies that the autocorrelation of short-horizon returns (daily) is increasing in the variance of noise, making it a measurable proxy. The key departure from a model such asCampbell and Kyle (1993)is that we model the inventory of noise traders as an AR(2) process, which is long-run mean-reverting but has short-run persistence in flows (⁠Δinventory). This process for noise naturally generates short-run momentum in returns along with long-run reversal, and provides a theoretical link between return autocorrelation and noise variance. What is the evidence for persistent noise flows?Barber et al. (2008)find that aggregate stock level order imbalance (flows) from individual investors are persistent for up to two months. Moreover, lagged flows positively predict future returns. These findings are consistent with the model in AppendixB, but would not obtain if noise followed a simpler AR(1) process. Further, a large literature documents that mutual fund flows are persistent for over a year.22Overall, the evidence strongly supports persistence in flows, contrary to an AR(1) assumption, but consistent with AR(2). Still, our measure is not perfect since return autocorrelation can be generated by additional mechanisms beyond noise trading.

Measured annually, the daily return autocorrelation has 56%cross-sectional correlation from yeartto yeart+1⁠. This implies that sorting on lagged autocorrelation generates meaningful spreads in future realized autocorrelation, and hence also noise variance. To test the prediction of an interaction betweenτand noise, we perform an independent double sort as inTable 4. The key predictions are that (1) alpha is decreasing in noise, (2) alpha is decreasing inτ⁠, which was already shown inTable 7, and (3) an interaction: higher noise increases the alpha associated withτ⁠, and vice versa. It is large at nearly 8%annualized with a 2.6t-statistic.Table 10repeats the exercise but now uses the FF5F benchmark model. Comparing the last columns ofTables 9and10, inclusion of the additional factors does substantially reduce the alpha associated with spread inτ⁠, but evidence for the interaction is nearly as strong. article.4. Conclusion:
In this paper, we show that the presence of noisy demand can lead to interesting asset pricing implications, even in an economy with symmetric information. As a consequence of investors’ preference for skewness, price as a function of quantities is convex in a neighborhood around zero if and only if the asset payoff is positively skewed. Applying Jensen’s inequality, a positively skewed asset can earn a negative expected return.

Our theory produces the skewness effect—a documented negative relationship between an asset’s skewness and its expected return—and offers an explanation for the idiosyncratic volatility puzzle. Finally, the theory predicts an interaction between the volatility and skewness effects—a higher value of idiosyncratic volatility strengthens the idiosyncratic skewness effect (and vice versa)—and an interaction between the third moment and noise effects. We find support for these predictions in the cross-section of stock returns.

While we focus on idiosyncratic moments, our theory offers additional testable predictions. First, Equation ( 7 ) also allows us to analyze comparative statics with respect to investor preferences. Note that the coefficient u ‴ ( w 0 ) u ′ ( w 0 ) can be expressed as ( − u ‴ ( w 0 ) u ″ ( w 0 ) ) ( − u ″ ( w 0 ) u ′ ( w 0 ) ) , which can be read as absolute prudence times absolute risk aversion. We predict that risk aversion and prudence can strengthen all our predictions. Second, the first part of Propositon 1 predicts that the shape of the price function is determined by the skewness of the underlying risky asset. One may test this prediction by recovering the price function from the limit order book (i.e., level 2 quote data). We leave these questions for future research.

B.3 Global Convexity/Concavity?

In this appendix, we prove the claim in Section2.2.1. The claim was that the demand function can not be globally convex/concave for a risky asset with bounded payoff.

Proof Suppose the risky asset’s payoff is bounded above by θ ― and below by θ ― ⁠ . We first show that demand approaches to negative infinity as price approaches the upper bound θ ― ⁠ , that is lim p → θ ― x ( p ) = − ∞ ⁠ . We argue by contradiction. Suppose x 0 ≡ lim inf p → θ ― x ( p ) > − ∞ ⁠ , then there exists a sequence, { p j } ⁠ , where p j → θ ― and x ( p j ) → x 0 ⁠ . Going back to the FOC ( 4 ), E [ u ′ ( w 0 + x ( p j ) ( θ ~ − p j ) ) ( θ ~ − p j ) ] = 0. Taking the limit as j → ∞ implies that E [ u ′ ( w 0 + x 0 ( θ ~ − θ ― ) ) ( θ ~ − θ ― ) ] = 0 , which is a contradiction since u ′ is strictly positive and ( θ ~ − θ ― ) is non-positive and at least some positive probability of a negative outcome. Similarly, we can prove that lim p → θ ― x ( p ) = + ∞ ⁠ . Note that the demand function x ( p ) is twice continuously differentiable, given our assumption of a thrice continuously differentiable utility function and Equation ( A5 ). Now we are ready to prove the claim that the demand function can not be globally convex or concave. We argue by contradiction. Suppose the demand function is globally convex. Then x ″ ( p ) is positive. Put differently, x ′ ( p ) is an increasing function. The demand function is downward sloping, x ′ ( p ) < 0 ⁠ . Now consider a small interval p ∈ ( θ 1 , θ ― ) where θ 1 ≡ θ ― − ϵ and ϵ > 0 ⁠ . It follows that x ′ ( θ 1 ) < x ′ ( p ) < 0 for all p ∈ ( θ 1 , θ ― ) ⁠ . Consider a sequence { p j } that converges to θ ― ⁠ . Using Newton-Leibniz rule, it follows that x ( θ 1 ) − x ( p j ) = ∫ θ 1 p j ( − x ′ ( p ) ) d p < ( p j − θ 1 ) | x ′ ( θ 1 ) | . Taking j to infinity, we obtain x ( θ 1 ) − lim inf p → θ ― x ( p ) ≤ ( θ ― − θ 1 ) | x ′ ( θ 1 ) | , which is a contradiction because RHS is a finite number while LHS is positive infinity since lim inf p → θ ― x ( p ) = − ∞ ⁠ . So the demand function can not be globally convex. A similar argument shows that the demand function can not be globally concave.■

B.4 Multiple Risky Assets with CRRA

Assume that the representative investor has a constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) utility function with risk aversion parameter γ ⁠ , in which case return is a sufficient statistic for wealth. Following Kraus and Litzenberger (1976) , we further assume that the investor only cares about the first three moments of returns as an approximation in the Taylor expansion. Specifically, let r ~ p be the return of a portfolio. The investor evaluates the portfolio according to the following preference, U ( r ~ p ) = E r ~ p − γ 2 V a r ( r ~ p ) + γ ′ E ( r ~ p − E r ~ p ) 3 , where γ ′ = γ ( γ + 1 ) / 6 ⁠ . Since the aggregate supply of the skewed asset is assumed to be zero, the market portfolio assigns zero weight on the skewed asset. However, the investor may hold the skewed asset because of market clearing in that asset. We write r ~ p = r ~ m + a r ~ ⁠ , where r ~ m is the return on the market portfolio, r ~ is the return on the skewed asset, and a is the weight on the skewed asset. Because of the independence assumption, r ~ m is independent of r ~ ⁠ . The investor chooses a to maximize E [ r ~ p ] − γ 2 V a r ( r ~ p ) + γ ′ E ( r ~ p − E [ r ~ p ] ) 3 .

The market clearing condition pins down the skewed asset’s price, equivalently the return.

Note that E ( r ~ p ) = E ( r ~ m ) + a E ( r ~ ) ⁠ , V a r ( r ~ p ) = V a r ( r ~ m ) + a 2 V a r ( r ~ ) ⁠ , and E ( r ~ p − E r ~ p ) 3 = E ( r ~ m − E r ~ m + a ( r ~ − E r ~ ) ) 3 = E ( r ~ m − E r ~ m ) 3 + a 3 E ( r ~ − E r ~ ) 3 ⁠ . Then the investor’s objective becomes E r ~ p − γ 2 V a r ( r ~ p ) + γ ′ E ( r ~ p − E r ~ p ) 3 = E r ~ m − γ 2 V a r ( r ~ m ) + γ ′ E ( r ~ m − E r ~ m ) 3 + ( a E r ~ − γ a 2 V a r ( r ~ ) + γ ′ a 3 E ( r ~ − E r ~ ) 3 ) .

Thus, the investor chooses a to maximize a E [ r ~ ] − γ 2 a 2 V a r ( r ~ ) + γ ′ a 3 E ( r ~ − E [ r ~ ] ) 3 .

In other words, the investor’s optimal choice in a multiple risky assets problems coincides with the optimal choice in the baseline model with a single risky asset. All our results in Section2carry over. In particular, a positively skewed asset can earn a negative expected return.

B.5 Autocorrelation and the Variance of Noise

In this appendix, we present a reduced-form model with noise traders to match the main empirical findings inBarber et al. (2008). The takeway is that the autocorrelation of returns is a good proxy for the variance of noise. This motivates our use of autocorrelation as a proxy in the main text.

In a financial market with a single risky asset and an infinite horizont∈{1,2,3,⋯}⁠, we assume the number of shares of the risky asset owned by noise traders at timetis denoted asyt⁠. SoΔyt=yt−yt−1is the actual shares from noise trading at timet⁠. Since our focus is not strategic trading, we take a reduced-form approach to model the price directly. Following the literature on dynamic asset pricing with noisy demand (see, e.g.,Campbell and Kyle 1993), we simply assume the price of the risky asset at timetis linear in the fundamental value and in noise trading. So, the change in the price is given by the change in the fundamental value and the change in the noise trading. That is,pt−pt−1=Δft+λΔyt⁠, whereΔftis the change in the fundamental value andλis a scalar that captures the sensitivity of how noise trading moves price. The dollar return isRt=pt−pt−1=Δft+λΔyt⁠. To focus on noise trading, we make another simplifying assumption that{Δft}is independent and identically distributed and it is independent of the noise trading.

One of the findings in Barber et al. (2008) is that noise trading is very persistent: a high level of noise trading today implies a high level of noise trading tomorrow. To model that, we write Δ y t = κ Δ y t − 1 + ϵ t with 0 < κ < 1 and ϵ t are white noise. 23 It follows that C o v ( R t , R t − 1 ) = λ 2 κ V a r ( Δ y t − 1 ) = λ 2 κ V a r ( Δ y ) , which is positive, consistent with Barber et al. ’s ( 2008 ) finding of return momentum. The autocorrelation of returns is given by C o v ( R t , R t − 1 ) V a r ( R ) = λ 2 κ V a r ( Δ y ) V a r ( Δ f ) + λ 2 V a r ( Δ y ) , (B7) which is an increasing function of the noise variance, V a r ( Δ y ) ⁠ . Put simply, a higher autocorrelation corresponds to a higher variance of noise.
